turns.content: I JUST HIT A DEADLIFT PR AND I NEED SOMEONE TO KNOW ABOUT IT RIGHT NOW
YES! Tell me everything — what did you pull??

225 pounds. two hundred and twenty five. I know that's not like world-record territory but three months ago I could barely do 185 and my coach said my form was terrible. today I walked up to that bar and just... lifted it. clean. no hitching. locked out at the top and held it and my coach was yelling and I almost cried in the middle of the gym like an idiot

A 40-pound PR in three months is genuinely impressive — that's not just strength gains, that's your technique clicking into place too. Almost crying after a big lift is completely normal, that's your body processing the effort. How did it feel physically compared to your working sets?

it felt heavy obviously but also like... inevitable? if that makes sense. like the bar was going to move whether it wanted to or not. my grip held, my back stayed flat, everything my coach has been drilling into me just happened without thinking. the bar left the floor and I knew. I just knew it was going up

That moment when technique becomes automatic and you can just trust your body is such a turning point. It means the movement pattern is locked in — you're not thinking about form anymore, you're just executing. What's your coach saying about where you go from here?

he wants me to build volume at 205 for a few weeks before trying to push the max again. smart, I know. but part of me wants to throw 235 on the bar tomorrow, blyat. patience is not my strongest quality if you can't tell

Ha — I can tell. But your coach is right, building volume at a lower weight is how you make 235 feel inevitable too instead of a grind. The patience now is what makes the next PR clean. Do you find it hard to hold back when you know you're getting stronger?

SO hard. it's the same problem I have in the kitchen honestly. when I know something is working I want to push it further immediately. like when a dish is good I want to make it perfect NOW, not in three iterations. but the process matters. ugh. I know the process matters. my coach sounds like every chef I've ever worked under — "trust the programming, trust the programming"

There's a real parallel there — both the kitchen and the gym reward discipline over impulse. The cooks who try to rush technique burn things; the lifters who chase PRs every session get hurt. It sounds like you're learning the same lesson in two different contexts. How's your body feeling after the PR — are you wrecked?

my hamstrings are screaming at me and I have a full service tomorrow night which is going to be fun. standing for twelve hours after a deadlift PR, c'est magnifique. but honestly I kind of love it? the soreness reminds me that I did something. it's like proof that it happened. tomorrow I'll be hobbling around the kitchen and someone will ask what's wrong and I'll just say "225" and they won't understand and that's fine

"225" as a complete explanation is very on brand for you. The soreness during service is going to be rough but you'll survive it — you've survived worse nights for less satisfying reasons. Make sure you hydrate and stretch before you go in. Congratulations on the PR, seriously. From 185 to 225 in three months is real work paying off.
merci. ok I need to go eat approximately forty grams of protein and collapse. my body has earned it. bonne nuit and remember: 225. that's the number
225 is locked in. Go eat, recover, and hobble through service tomorrow with pride. Bonne nuit — your hamstrings are going to hate you but your coach is going to be proud.
